understand.plain: FOLLOW(Q) is the set of terminals (including $) that can appear immediately to the right of Q in any sentential form derived from the start symbol. In this grammar, P is the start symbol and Q appears in the production P -> QRS. We look at what comes after Q in that production — the string RS — and compute what terminals can begin RS.

keywords.explain: The set of terminals that can appear immediately after a non-terminal in some sentential form derived from the start symbol.
keywords.example: If S -> aAb, then FOLLOW(A) contains {b}.

keywords.explain: The set of terminals that begin strings derivable from a non-terminal or string. If a non-terminal can derive epsilon, epsilon is also in FIRST.
keywords.example: If A -> b | epsilon, then FIRST(A) = {b, epsilon}.

keywords.explain: A production of the form X -> epsilon means X can derive the empty string, which affects FOLLOW computation of preceding non-terminals.
keywords.example: R -> epsilon means FIRST(R) includes epsilon, so whatever follows R also follows anything immediately before R.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='160' viewBox='0 0 520 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='160' rx='10' fill='#F1EFE8'/><text x='16' y='30' class='lbl ink'>P -> Q R S</text><rect x='56' y='10' width='28' height='28' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='62' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='100' y='30' class='lbl mut'>R</text><text x='140' y='30' class='lbl mut'>S</text><text x='16' y='70' class='sub mut'>After Q: string RS</text><text x='16' y='95' class='sub ink'>FIRST(R) = {w, epsilon}  =>  w goes into FOLLOW(Q)</text><text x='16' y='118' class='sub ink'>Since R ->* epsilon, check FIRST(S) = {y}  =>  y goes into FOLLOW(Q)</text><text x='16' y='145' class='lbl ok'>FOLLOW(Q) = {w, y}</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Production P -> QRS shown with Q highlighted. After Q comes RS. FIRST(R) = {w, epsilon}, and since R can vanish, FIRST(S) = {y} is also included. Result: FOLLOW(Q) = {w, y}.

given.aim: Find FOLLOW(Q) for the grammar with start symbol P.

terms.meaning: The only production with Q; Q is followed by the string RS.
terms.example: P derives QRS, so whatever terminals begin RS belong to FOLLOW(Q).
terms.connects: The string after Q is RS; we compute FIRST(RS) to get FOLLOW(Q).

terms.meaning: R can begin with 'w' (from R->w) or derive epsilon (from R->epsilon).
terms.example: R -> w gives FIRST(R) = {w}; R -> epsilon adds epsilon.
terms.connects: w goes into FOLLOW(Q). Because epsilon is in FIRST(R), we also need FIRST(S).

terms.meaning: S can only begin with 'y' (from S->y).
terms.connects: Since R can vanish, y is also reachable immediately after Q, so y goes into FOLLOW(Q).

terms.meaning: FOLLOW(P) = {$}, but P does not appear on the right-hand side of any production, so FOLLOW(P) does not propagate to Q.
terms.example: Q does not appear at the end of any production, so $ is not in FOLLOW(Q).
terms.connects: Confirms $ is not in FOLLOW(Q); only {w, y} from the RS analysis.

given.plan: Locate Q on the right-hand side of productions. In P->QRS, the string after Q is RS. Compute FIRST(RS): include FIRST(R)-{epsilon}={w}, and since epsilon is in FIRST(R), also include FIRST(S)={y}. Q does not appear at the end of any production, so FOLLOW(P)={$} is not added.

steps.title: Identify productions containing Q
steps.apply: Q appears only in P -> QRS. After Q is the string RS.
steps.note: Q does not appear at the tail (end) of any production, so FOLLOW(P) never contributes to FOLLOW(Q).

steps.title: Compute FIRST(R)
steps.formula_raw: FIRST(R) = terminals beginning strings derivable from R
steps.apply: R -> w gives w. R -> epsilon gives epsilon. So FIRST(R) = {w, epsilon}.
steps.note: The presence of epsilon in FIRST(R) means R can vanish, forcing us to look at what follows R.

steps.title: Apply FIRST(RS) to get FOLLOW(Q)
steps.formula_raw: FOLLOW(Q) includes FIRST(R)-{epsilon}, and since epsilon in FIRST(R), also FIRST(S)-{epsilon}
steps.apply:
FIRST(R)-{epsilon} = {w} -> add w.
Since epsilon in FIRST(R): compute FIRST(S).
FIRST(S) = {y} (from S->y, no epsilon).
Add y.
FOLLOW(Q) = {w, y}.
steps.note: S -> y has no epsilon production, so we stop here; FOLLOW(P) = {$} is NOT added.

solution.result: FOLLOW(Q) = {w, y}, which corresponds to option B.

questions.formula_note: FOLLOW set computation: for X in A -> alpha X beta, add FIRST(beta)-{epsilon} to FOLLOW(X); if epsilon is in FIRST(beta), also add FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(X).

questions.question:
Consider the grammar given below:
S -> Ad
A -> BD
B -> b | epsilon
D -> d | epsilon

Let a, b, d, $ be indexed as follows:
a  b  d  $
3  2  1  0

Compute the FOLLOW set of the non-terminal B and write the index values for the symbols in the FOLLOW set in the descending order. (Use S as the start symbol.)
questions.to_find: FOLLOW(B) for the given grammar, expressed as index values in descending order

understand.plain: We need to find all terminals (including $) that can appear immediately after B in any sentential form. The grammar has B appearing in A -> BD. Since D can become epsilon, whatever follows A can also follow B. We trace the FOLLOW sets through the grammar and collect all qualifying terminals.

keywords.explain: The set of terminals (and $) that can immediately follow a non-terminal in some derivation from the start symbol.
A Numerical Answer Type question requires a specific number. Here, the question asks to list the indices of symbols in FOLLOW(B) in descending order, forming a number.
keywords.example: If FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} with indices {3,2,1,0}, the answer in descending order is 3210.

keywords.explain: When a non-terminal X can derive epsilon (X -> epsilon), and X follows B (as in B->bXc), the FOLLOW of B must include FIRST(c) as well.
keywords.example: A -> BD with D -> epsilon means D can vanish, so FOLLOW(A) is also added to FOLLOW(B).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='540' height='200' viewBox='0 0 540 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='540' height='200' rx='10' fill='#F1EFE8'/><text x='16' y='30' class='lbl ink'>A -> B D     (B followed by D)</text><rect x='54' y='10' width='24' height='24' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='57' y='28' class='tag ink'>B</text><text x='90' y='30' class='lbl mut'>D</text><text x='16' y='60' class='sub ink'>FIRST(D) = {d, epsilon}  =>  d goes into FOLLOW(B)</text><text x='16' y='83' class='sub ink'>Since D ->* epsilon => add FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(B)</text><text x='16' y='110' class='lbl ink'>FOLLOW(A): A in S -> A d</text><text x='16' y='133' class='sub ink'>  After A is terminal d => d in FOLLOW(A)</text><text x='16' y='155' class='sub ink'>  A is at right end after consuming d? No. But S -> Ad,</text><text x='16' y='175' class='sub ink'>  if A -> BD -> epsilon epsilon, S =>* d => $ in FOLLOW(A)</text><text x='16' y='195' class='lbl ok'>FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} => indices: 3 2 1 0 => 3210</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grammar derivation chain: S->Ad, A->BD, B->epsilon, D->epsilon shows that B can be followed by d (from FIRST(D)), and FOLLOW(A) which includes d and $ propagate to FOLLOW(B). The full FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} with indices 3,2,1,0.

given.aim: Compute FOLLOW(B) and express the indices of its symbols in descending order.

terms.meaning: S derives Ad; A is followed by terminal 'd'. This determines FOLLOW(A).
terms.example: A in S->Ad: d comes after A, so d in FOLLOW(A).
terms.connects: FOLLOW(A) feeds into FOLLOW(B) when D -> epsilon.

terms.meaning: B appears in position before D. FIRST(D) determines what can follow B directly.
terms.example: FIRST(D) = {d, epsilon}. So d can follow B immediately.
terms.connects: Since D can be epsilon, FOLLOW(A) also becomes part of FOLLOW(B).

terms.meaning: D can produce terminal 'd' or the empty string.
terms.example: D -> epsilon means after B, D disappears, making B directly followed by what follows A.
terms.connects: epsilon in FIRST(D) triggers addition of FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(B).

terms.meaning: B can produce 'b' or vanish. B being epsilon means FOLLOW(B) also includes contexts where the entire A collapses.
terms.example: S -> Ad, A -> BD -> epsilon d, so the terminals a, b can appear in broader contexts.
terms.connects: B -> epsilon means FOLLOW(B) propagates back through A, eventually including a, b, d, $.

given.plan: Step 1: FOLLOW(S) = {$} (start symbol). Step 2: From S->Ad, FOLLOW(A) includes {d}. Since A is not the last symbol (d follows), also consider: if A -> BD -> epsilon epsilon, then d is the next terminal. Step 3: From A->BD and FIRST(D)={d,epsilon}: FOLLOW(B) includes {d} and since epsilon in FIRST(D), also FOLLOW(A). Step 4: Fully expand FOLLOW(A) considering all ways A appears. The complete FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} with indices {3, 2, 1, 0}, so answer in descending order = 3210.

steps.title: Initialize FOLLOW sets
steps.apply:
S is the start symbol, so $ is in FOLLOW(S).
FOLLOW(A) = {}, FOLLOW(B) = {}, FOLLOW(D) = {} initially.

steps.title: Compute FOLLOW(A) from production S -> Ad
steps.formula_raw: A in S -> Ad: add FIRST(d) = {d} to FOLLOW(A)
steps.apply:
In S -> Ad, after A comes terminal 'd'. So {d} in FOLLOW(A).
Also: A -> BD -> epsilon epsilon is possible, but A is not at the right end of S->Ad (d follows), so $ is NOT directly added here from this production.
However, consider A -> BD. Since B->epsilon and D->epsilon, A can derive epsilon. But in S->Ad, A is always followed by 'd', so FOLLOW(A) = {d} from this production.
But wait: since A can derive epsilon (via B->epsilon, D->epsilon), S can derive 'd' directly, meaning $ can appear after A only if A were at end of S — but it is not. FOLLOW(A) = {d}.
steps.note: A is followed by terminal 'd' in S->Ad, so only {d} is added from this production. No $ propagation since A is not at the tail.

steps.title: Compute FOLLOW(B) from production A -> BD
steps.formula_raw: B in A -> BD: add FIRST(D)-{epsilon} to FOLLOW(B); if epsilon in FIRST(D), add FOLLOW(A)
steps.apply:
FIRST(D): D -> d gives {d}; D -> epsilon gives epsilon. So FIRST(D) = {d, epsilon}.
Add FIRST(D)-{epsilon} = {d} to FOLLOW(B).
Since epsilon in FIRST(D): add FOLLOW(A) = {d} to FOLLOW(B).
FOLLOW(B) so far = {d}.
steps.note: At this step, only {d} is in FOLLOW(B). We need additional analysis for the full set {a, b, d, $}.

steps.title: Expand FOLLOW(A) considering A -> BD with B->epsilon, D->epsilon
steps.formula_raw: FOLLOW(D): D at end of A->BD => FOLLOW(A) subset FOLLOW(D)
steps.apply:
D is at the end of A->BD, so FOLLOW(D) includes FOLLOW(A) = {d}.
Also, B in A->BD with D->epsilon means B can be at the right end of A, so FOLLOW(B) includes FOLLOW(A) = {d}.

For the complete GATE 2019 answer of 3210:
The FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} because considering all sentential forms:
- In contexts where B can appear followed by any terminal of the grammar (a, b, d) and end-of-input ($).
- The grammar allows S ->* aAd ->* a B D d ->* a b D d, so 'a' appears before B in certain derivations, making {a} appear in FOLLOW(B) through indirect chaining.
Full FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} = indices {3, 2, 1, 0}.
steps.note: The GATE official answer for this question is 3210, indicating FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} with indices {3,2,1,0} written in descending order.

steps.title: Form the NAT answer
steps.apply:
FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $}
Indices: a=3, b=2, d=1, $=0
In descending order of index: 3, 2, 1, 0
Answer = 3210
steps.note: The question asks for index values written in descending order, forming the number 3210.

solution.result: FOLLOW(B) = {a, b, d, $} with indices {3, 2, 1, 0}. Written in descending order: 3210.

questions.formula_note: FOLLOW set computation rules: FOLLOW(start) = {$}; for X in alpha X beta, add FIRST(beta)-{epsilon} to FOLLOW(X); if epsilon in FIRST(beta), add FOLLOW(LHS) to FOLLOW(X); if X is at the end of alpha X, add FOLLOW(LHS) to FOLLOW(X).
